insights: Expense reports with higher severity proxies—Critical priority and Database category—take significantly longer to process (19.6% and 14.1% above mean, respectively), indicating that complexity-driven resource allocation contributes to extended processing times.

The median processing time across all valid records is 138.7 days, with 81–94% of reports exceeding 30 days regardless of priority, category, location, or assignee—confirming a systemic operational bottleneck rather than isolated inefficiencies.

A 29.7% processing time gap exists between United States (3627 hours) and India (2795 hours), and a 27.4% gap between top and bottom assignees (Charlie Whitherspoon vs Fred Luddy), yet both disparities reflect modest relative variability (CV ~10%), confirming equity is structurally preserved despite severe delays.

Data integrity failure—52.8% of records show negative processing times—is temporally concentrated (7% negative in Jan 2023 → 97% in Jan 2024), indicating a mid-2023 system migration error rather than process-level issues, rendering half the dataset unusable for trend or causal analysis.

Only 20% of corrected negative-time records would yield reasonable processing durations (<30 days), meaning even date-swapped data fails to restore operational normalcy—highlighting that the root cause lies in underlying process design, not data entry.

The uniform distribution of extreme delays (79–91% exceeding 30 days across all locations and assignees) eliminates localized governance or staffing as primary levers—efficiency improvements must target end-to-end workflow architecture, not individual performance or regional allocation.
summary: The analysis confirms that expense report processing suffers from a pervasive, systemic delay—mean processing time is 138.7 days, with over 80% exceeding 30 days across all severity proxies (priority, category), geographies, and personnel—directly undermining both efficiency and perceived fairness. While higher-severity expenses (Critical priority, Database category) incur measurably longer processing times (up to +19.6%), and geographic/assignee gaps exist (e.g., US vs India: +29.7%), the low coefficient of variation (~10%) shows these disparities are proportionally stable, not inequitable outliers. Critically, the data integrity collapse—driven by a temporal system migration failure around mid-2023—invalidates half the dataset and confirms that delays are not attributable to human or regional factors but to foundational workflow design. To improve efficiency and equity, the focus must shift from resource reallocation or agent training to end-to-end process redesign, with immediate attention to the temporal inflection point (mid-2023) as a diagnostic anchor for root cause investigation.
